subject: Physics
q: A hydrogen atom in ground state is given an energy of $10.2 \mathrm{eV}$. How many spectral lines will be emitted due to transition of electrons?
question: A hydrogen atom in ground state is given an energy of $10.2 \mathrm{eV}$. How many spectral lines will be emitted due to transition of electrons?
solution: Hydrogen will be in first excited state therefore it will emit one spectral line corresponding to transition b/w energy level 2 to 1

q: Which of the following figure represents the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>R</mi><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mi>A</mi></math> (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of a nucleus and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo></math> its mass number)
question: Which of the following figure represents the variation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>R</mi><msub><mi>R</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfrac></mfenced></math> with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ln</mi><mi>A</mi></math> (if <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of a nucleus and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo></math> its mass number)
solution:
We know the relation between radius and atomic mass number is R=R0A13 ⇒lnR=lnR0+13lnA

⇒lnRR0=13lnA 
It is similar to y=mx which is equation of line passing through origin.

E=13.6111-122 eV  ...(1)
For Helium:
E=13.6221n12-1n22 eV ...(2)
From equation (1) and (2)
13.6 34=13.6 4 1n12-1n22
1n12-1n22=316

subject: Physics
q: The transition from the state $n=4$ to $n=3$ in a hydrogen like atom results in ultraviolet radiation. Infrared radiation will be obtained in the transition from
question: The transition from the state $n=4$ to $n=3$ in a hydrogen like atom results in ultraviolet radiation. Infrared radiation will be obtained in the transition from
solution:
IR corresponds to least value of $\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$
i.e. from Paschen, Bracket and Pfund series. Thus the transition corresponds to $5 \rightarrow 3$.

subject: Physics
q: The atomic hydrogen emits a line spectrum consisting of various series. Which series of hydrogen atomic spectra is lying in the visible region?
question: The atomic hydrogen emits a line spectrum consisting of various series. Which series of hydrogen atomic spectra is lying in the visible region?
solution: The transition from higher states to n=2 lead to the emission of radiation with wavelengths 656.3 nm and 365.0 nm. These wavelengths fall in the visible region and constitute the Balmer series.

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Emission of electrons in photoelectric effect can be suppressed by applying a sufficiently negative electron potential to the photoemissive substance.
Reason (R) : A negative electric potential, which stops the emission of electrons from the surface of a photoemissive substance, varies linearly with frequency of incident radiation.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Emission of electrons in photoelectric effect can be suppressed by applying a sufficiently negative electron potential to the photoemissive substance.
Reason (R) : A negative electric potential, which stops the emission of electrons from the surface of a photoemissive substance, varies linearly with frequency of incident radiation.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true and \((\mathbf{R})\) is the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
Both \((\mathbf{A})\) and \((\mathbf{R})\) are true but \((\mathbf{R})\) is not the correct explanation of \((\mathbf{A})\)
solution:
Assertion (A) is true because a sufficiently negative potential can repel and stop the emitted electrons from leaving the surface.
Reason (R) is also true because the stopping potential \(V_{\text {stop }}\) varies linearly with the frequency \(\nu\) of the incident light \(\left(V_{\text {stop }}=\frac{h}{e} \nu-\frac{\phi}{e}\right)\).
However, (R) does not directly explain why a negative potential suppresses electron emission; it only shows how much potential is needed for different frequencies.
Therefore, both (A) and (R) are true, but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A).

q:
In Li++,electron in first Bohr orbit is excited to a level by a radiation of wavelength λ.When the ion gets de excited to the ground state in all possible ways (including intermediate emissions), a total of six spectral lines are observed. What is the value of  λ ?

(Given:  h=6.63×10-34 J s ;c=3×108 m s-1 )
question:
In Li++,electron in first Bohr orbit is excited to a level by a radiation of wavelength λ.When the ion gets de excited to the ground state in all possible ways (including intermediate emissions), a total of six spectral lines are observed. What is the value of  λ ?

(Given:  h=6.63×10-34 J s ;c=3×108 m s-1 )
solution:
Total Number of possible spectral lines that can be emmited when atom de-excites from state n to groundstate is C2n
∴ nC2=6⇒n(n-1)2=6⇒n=4
1λ=R z2112-142=1.097×107×9×1-116
⇒λ=10.8 nm

subject: Physics
q:
A monochromatic light is incident on a hydrogen sample in ground state. Hydrogen atoms absorb a fraction of light and subsequently emit radiation of six different wavelengths. The frequency of incident light is x×1015 Hz. The value of x is ________
(Given h=4.25×10-15 eVs)
question:
A monochromatic light is incident on a hydrogen sample in ground state. Hydrogen atoms absorb a fraction of light and subsequently emit radiation of six different wavelengths. The frequency of incident light is x×1015 Hz. The value of x is ________
(Given h=4.25×10-15 eVs)
solution:
Let the higher state be n.

The number of wavelengths emitted is given by,

n(n-1)2=6⇒n2-n-12=0⇒n-4(n+3)=0⇒n=4

For hydrogen atom,
hν=13.611-116=13.6×1516eV⇒ν=Eh=13.6×15×1.6×10-1916×6.62×10-34=3×1015Hz

subject: Physics
q:
In Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment, only a few alpha particles rebound back because
A. The size of gold nucleus is very small as compared to the size of gold atom.
B. Alpha particle and gold nucleus have equal charge.
C. The impact parameter is minimum for a few alpha particles.
D. A few alpha particles have very high kinetic energy.
E. Only a few alpha particles undergo head-on collision with the nuclei.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment, only a few alpha particles rebound back because
A. The size of gold nucleus is very small as compared to the size of gold atom.
B. Alpha particle and gold nucleus have equal charge.
C. The impact parameter is minimum for a few alpha particles.
D. A few alpha particles have very high kinetic energy.
E. Only a few alpha particles undergo head-on collision with the nuclei.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
In Rutherford's alpha-particle scattering experiment, the rebounding of alpha particles (scattering angle $\approx 180^{\circ}$) occurs when they undergo a head-on collision with the gold nucleus.

A head-on collision corresponds to an impact parameter close to zero. Since the size of the gold nucleus ($\approx 10^{-15}$ m) is extremely small compared to the size of the gold atom ($\approx 10^{-10}$ m), the effective target area is very small. Consequently, the probability of an alpha particle having a minimum impact parameter is very low.

Thus, only a few alpha particles undergo head-on collisions and rebound back. This makes statements A, C, and E correct.

Statement B is incorrect because the charge of an alpha particle is $+2e$ while the charge of a gold nucleus is $+79e$.

Statement D is incorrect because the alpha particles emitted from the radioactive source have nearly the same kinetic energy.

Answer: A, C, E Only

subject: Physics
q:
The angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is proportional to :
(Where $r$ is the radius of orbit of electron)
question:
The angular momentum of an electron in a hydrogen atom is proportional to :
(Where $r$ is the radius of orbit of electron)
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{Kq}_1 \mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{r}^2}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{mv}^2 \mathrm{r}^2=K \mathrm{~K}_1 \mathrm{q}_2 \mathrm{r} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{L}^2}{\mathrm{~m}}=K \mathrm{Kq}_1 \mathrm{q}_2 \mathrm{r} \\ & \mathrm{L} \propto \sqrt{\mathrm{r}}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: As an electron makes a transition from an excited state to the ground state of a hydrogen-like atom/ion
question: As an electron makes a transition from an excited state to the ground state of a hydrogen-like atom/ion
options: kinetic energy and total energy decrease but potential energy increases
its kinetic energy increases but potential energy and total energy decrease
kinetic energy, potential energy and total energy decrease
kinetic energy decreases, potential energy increases but total energy remains same
solution:
U=-e24πε0r, U= potential energy, K=e24πε0r
K=kinetic energy. E=U+K=-e28πε0r, E=total energy∴ As electron de-excites from an excited state to ground state, K increases, but U and E decrease.

q: The momentum of an electron revolving in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> orbit is given by : (Symbols have their usual meanings)
question: The momentum of an electron revolving in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mrow><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi><mo> </mo></mrow></msup></math> orbit is given by : (Symbols have their usual meanings)
solution:
According to Bohr's postulates, angular momentum of an electron in an orbit is given by,

L=mvr=nh2π

Therefore, linear momentum p=Lr=nh2πr

q: The electron of a hydrogen atom makes a transition from the $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ orbit to the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit. For large $\mathrm{n}$ the wavelength of the emitted radiation is proportional to
question: The electron of a hydrogen atom makes a transition from the $(n+1)^{\text {th }}$ orbit to the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit. For large $\mathrm{n}$ the wavelength of the emitted radiation is proportional to
solution:
If $n_1=n$ and $n_2=n+1$ Maximum wavelength $\lambda_{\max }=\frac{n^2\left(n+1^2\right)}{(2 n+1 R)}$
Therefore, for large $\mathrm{n}, \lambda_{\max } \propto n^3$

subject: Physics
q: During the transition of electron from state A to state C of a Bohr atom, the wavelength of emitted radiation is $2000 Å$ and it becomes $6000 Å$ when the electron jumps from state B to state C. Then the wavelength of the radiation emitted during the transition of electrons from state A to state B is
question: During the transition of electron from state A to state C of a Bohr atom, the wavelength of emitted radiation is $2000 Å$ and it becomes $6000 Å$ when the electron jumps from state B to state C. Then the wavelength of the radiation emitted during the transition of electrons from state A to state B is
solution: $\frac{h c}{\lambda_1}=E_0 z^2\left(\frac{1}{n_c^2}-\frac{1}{n_A^2}\right)$ ....(i) And $\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}=E_0 z^2\left(\frac{1}{n_c^2}-\frac{1}{n_B^2}\right)$ So for $A$ and $B$ ....(ii) $\frac{h c}{\lambda_3}=E_0 z^2\left(\frac{1}{n_B^2}-\frac{1}{n_A^2}\right)$ Clearly subtracting equation (ii) from equation (i) $\begin{aligned} & h c\left[\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right]=E_0 z^2\left[\frac{1}{n_B^2}-\frac{1}{n_A^2}\right]=\frac{h c}{\lambda_3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda_3}=\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2} \Rightarrow \frac{1}{\lambda_3}=\frac{(6000-2000)}{6000 \times 2000}=\frac{1}{3000} \\ & \lambda_3=3000 Å \end{aligned}$

q: A free electron of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> energy collides with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion. This results in the formation of a hydrogen atom in the first excited state and a photon is released. Find the frequency of the emitted photon. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: A free electron of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>eV</mi></math> energy collides with a <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mo>+</mo></msup></math> ion. This results in the formation of a hydrogen atom in the first excited state and a photon is released. Find the frequency of the emitted photon. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>34</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
By Energy conservation
K.E.e=T.E.H+ +EPhoton
2.6=-13.64+hf
hf=6eV
f=6×1.6×10-196.626×10-34
f=1.45×1015 Hz=1.45×109 MHz

subject: Physics
q: An electron in the hydrogen atom initially in the fourth excited state makes a transition to $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ energy state by emitting a photon of energy 2.86 eV. The integer value of $n$ will be ____.
question: An electron in the hydrogen atom initially in the fourth excited state makes a transition to $\mathrm{n}^{\text {th }}$ energy state by emitting a photon of energy 2.86 eV. The integer value of $n$ will be ____.
solution:
$\mathrm{E}=13.6\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}\right)$
$2.86=13.6\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}-\frac{1}{5^2}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\mathrm{n}^2}=0.21+\frac{1}{2.5} \\ & \mathrm{n}^2=4 \\ & \mathrm{n}=2\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Considering Bohr's atomic model for hydrogen atom :
(A) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state.
(B) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its second excited state.
(C) the energy of H atom in its ground state is same as that of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion for its ground state.
(D) the energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Considering Bohr's atomic model for hydrogen atom :
(A) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state.
(B) the energy of H atom in ground state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its second excited state.
(C) the energy of H atom in its ground state is same as that of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion for its ground state.
(D) the energy of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ion in its first excited state is same as that for $\mathrm{Li}^{++}$ion in its ground state
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{E} \propto \frac{\mathrm{Z}}{\mathrm{n}^2} \\ & \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{H}}=1 \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}=2 \quad \mathrm{Z}_{\mathrm{Li}^{+2}}=3\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & 1^{\text {st }} \text { excited state } \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=2 \\ & 2^{\text {nd }} \text { excited state } \Rightarrow \mathrm{n}=3\end{aligned}$
From the given statements only A \& B are correct.

question: Considering the Bohr model of hydrogen like atoms, the ratio of the ratio of the radius $5^{\text {th }}$ orbit of the electron in $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is
solution:
$\mathrm{r}=\mathrm{r} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{n}^2}{2}$
for $\mathrm{Li}^{2+}$
$r_5=r \cdot \frac{25}{3}$
for $\mathrm{He}^{+}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{r}_5=\mathrm{r} \cdot \frac{25}{2} \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{Li}^{2+}}}{\mathrm{r}_{\mathrm{He}^{+}}}=\frac{2}{3}
\end{aligned}$

=10 keV

q: A particular hydrogen - like ion emits the radiation of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> when it makes transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The frequency of radiation emitted in transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=3</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> _____.
question: A particular hydrogen - like ion emits the radiation of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> when it makes transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo></math> The frequency of radiation emitted in transition from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=3</mo></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>x</mi><mn>9</mn></mfrac><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>15</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi><mo>,</mo></math> when <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo></math> _____.
solution:
For Hydrogen like atom, energy emitted is E=-13.6Z21ni2-1nf2

⇒E=C1nf2-1ni2

⇒hν=C1nf2-1ni2

Therefore, the  ratiov1v2=1nf2-1ni22-11nf2-1ni23-1

=11-1411-19=3489

=34×98

⇒v1v2=2732

⇒v2=3227v1=3227×3×1015 Hz=329×1015 Hz

Hence, x=32.

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mo>∝</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>x</mi></msup></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
question: An electron revolving in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>n</mi><mtext>th </mtext></msup></math> Bohr orbit has magnetic moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub></math>. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>μ</mi><mi>n</mi></msub><mo> </mo><mo>∝</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>n</mi><mi>x</mi></msup></math>, the value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is:
solution:
Magnetic moment of electron is given by, μn=ehn4πme.

Clearly, μn∝ n1. Therefore, x=1.

subject: Physics
q: If Rydberg's constant is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>, the longest wavelength of radiation in Paschen series will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
question: If Rydberg's constant is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math>, the longest wavelength of radiation in Paschen series will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>R</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>=</mo></math>______.
solution:
In the Paschen radiation series, the longest wavelength corresponds to the transition between n=3 and n=4.

The formula for the wavelength in the given transition can be written as, 

1λ=RZ21n12-1n22   ...1

From equation (1), it follows that

1λ=RZ2132-142=RZ219-116=7RZ29×16⇒λ=1447R

 Hence, α=144.

subject: Physics
q: If an alpha particle with energy 7.7 MeV is bombarded on a thin gold foil, the closest distance from nucleus it can reach is $\_\_\_\_$ m. (Atomic number of gold $=79$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in SI units)
question: If an alpha particle with energy 7.7 MeV is bombarded on a thin gold foil, the closest distance from nucleus it can reach is $\_\_\_\_$ m. (Atomic number of gold $=79$ and $\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{\mathrm{o}}}=9 \times 10^{9}$ in SI units)
solution:
At closest approach, KE converts to PE:
$KE = \frac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0} \times \frac{(2e)(Ze)}{r_{min}}$
$r_{min} = \frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 79 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}{7.7 \times 10^6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}$
$= \frac{9 \times 2 \times 79 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}{7.7} \times 10^{3}$
$= \frac{2275.2}{7.7} \times 10^{-16} = 2.95 \times 10^{-14}$ m

q: The radius of third stationary orbit of electron for Bohr's atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The radius of fourth stationary orbit will be:
question: The radius of third stationary orbit of electron for Bohr's atom is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math>. The radius of fourth stationary orbit will be:
solution:
From Bohr's atomic model, it follows that

rn=n2Zr1   ...1

Following equation (1), it can be written that

r4r3=4232⇒r4=169R

subject: Physics
q: Orbits of a particle moving in a circle are such that the perimeter of the orbit equals an integer number of de-Broglie wavelengths of the particle. For a charged particle moving in a plane perpendicular to a magnetic field, the radius of the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbital will therefore be proportional to :
question: Orbits of a particle moving in a circle are such that the perimeter of the orbit equals an integer number of de-Broglie wavelengths of the particle. For a charged particle moving in a plane perpendicular to a magnetic field, the radius of the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbital will therefore be proportional to :
solution:
According to the question,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& 2 \pi \mathrm{r}=\mathrm{n} \lambda=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{\mathrm{mv}} \\
& \text { or } \mathrm{mvr}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi} \text { or } \mathrm{mv}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r}} \\
& \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{qv}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \text { or, } \mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{B}}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}}{\mathrm{r}}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{r} \cdot \mathrm{r}} \\
& \text { or, } \mathrm{r}^2=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{qB}} \\
& \text { or, } \mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi \mathrm{qB}}} \\
& \text { i.e., } \mathrm{r} \propto \mathrm{n}^{1 / 2}
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
An electron of a hydrogen like atom, having Z = 4, jumps from 4th energy state to 2nd energy state, The energy released in this process, will be: (Given Rch = 13.6 eV)

Where R = Rydberg

constant c = Speed of light in vacuum

h = Planck's constant
question:
An electron of a hydrogen like atom, having Z = 4, jumps from 4th energy state to 2nd energy state, The energy released in this process, will be: (Given Rch = 13.6 eV)

Where R = Rydberg

constant c = Speed of light in vacuum

h = Planck's constant
solution:
According to Bohr's atomic theory, whenever an electron jumps from one orbit to another, there is always an emission or absorption energy in terms of emission or absorption of photons.

The formula to calculate the energy ∆E of the emitted or absorbed photon when an electron jumps from a state with quantum number n1 to another with quantum number n2 is given by

∆E= -RchZ21ni2-1nf2....................(1)

where, R is Rydberg's constant, h is Planck's constant, c is the speed of light and Z is the atomic number.

Substitute the values of the given parameters into equation (1) to calculate the required energy.

∆E=-13.6×42142-122 eV= -13.6×16×116-14 eV= 40.8 eV

subject: Physics
q: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
question: The energy required to remove the electron from a singly ionized Helium atom is $2.2$ times the energy required to remove an electron from Helium atom. The total energy required to ionize the Helium atom completely is:
solution:
Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from singly ionized helium atom $=\frac{(13.6) Z^2}{1^2}=54.4 \mathrm{eV}$ $(\because \mathrm{Z}=2)$ Energy required to remove $\mathrm{e}^{-}$from helium atom $=x \mathrm{eV}$
According to question, $54.4 \mathrm{eV}=2.2 \mathrm{x} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}$ $=24.73 \mathrm{eV}$
Therefore, energy required to ionize helium atom $=(54.4+24.73) \mathrm{eV}=79.12 \mathrm{eV}$

subject: Physics
q: The longest wavelength associated with Paschen series is : (Given $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{SI}$ unit)
question: The longest wavelength associated with Paschen series is : (Given $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{H}}=1.097 \times 10^7 \mathrm{SI}$ unit)
solution:
For longest wavelength in Paschen's series:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_1^2}-\frac{1}{\mathrm{n}_2^2}\right]$
For longest $\mathrm{n}_1=3$
$\mathrm{n}_2=4$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{(3)^2}-\frac{1}{(4)^2}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{16}\right] \\ & \frac{1}{\lambda}=\mathrm{R}\left[\frac{16-9}{16 \times 9}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \lambda=\frac{16 \times 9}{7 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{16 \times 9}{7 \times 1.097 \times 10^7} \\ & \lambda=1.876 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Which of the plots shown in the figure represents speed $(v)$ of the electron in a hydrogen atom as a function of the principal quantum number $(n)$ ?
question: Which of the plots shown in the figure represents speed $(v)$ of the electron in a hydrogen atom as a function of the principal quantum number $(n)$ ?
solution:
Velocity of electron in $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit of hydrogen atom is given by :
$$
V_n=\frac{2 \pi K Z e^2}{n h}
$$
Substituting the values we get,
$$
V_n=\frac{2.2 \times 10^6}{n} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \quad \text { or } \quad V_n \propto \frac{1}{n}
$$
As principal quantum number increases, velocity decreases.
